title: Is diesel fuel significantly pricier in Latvia than in neighbouring countries?
content:
Since the escalation of the conflict in the Middle East over the past two months, oil prices have risen sharply, followed accordingly by an increase in fuel prices – especially diesel.

However, this has also raised questions and doubts about whether the price increase across the European Union has been similar and why prices are higher or lower in one country or another.

In social media, criticism occasionally appears claiming that fuel is significantly cheaper in Lithuania and Estonia than in Latvia, with suggestions that the price difference is as much as 20-30 euro cents. Delfi explains whether fuel in Latvia is indeed significantly more expensive compared with neighbouring countries.

Where is it pricier, and where is it cheaper?

The Chair of the Board of the Latvian Fuel Traders Association (LDTA), Ieva Ligere, told Delfi that, just as in Latvia, prices also vary between filling stations in Lithuania and Estonia, which is explained by competition in specific locations.

“The greater the competition in a particular location, the lower the

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Magyar in Tusk’s footsteps: what it takes to restore EU funds
content:
The future Hungarian Prime Minister Péter Magyar is in Brussels today for talks with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen on unlocking EU funds. In a comparable situation two years earlier stood Donald Tusk.

Returning to power in Poland after nearly a decade in December 2023, Donald Tusk pledged to “bring Poland back to Europe” after the years of Law and Justice (PiS) rule. During his visit to Brussels that same month, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen praised him for “placing the rule of law at the very top of his new government’s agenda.”

After this year’s elections in Hungary, the head of the European Commission struck a similarly optimistic tone. “Hungary chose Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary,” she wrote on the X platform shortly after the first preliminary results were announced.

The task facing Péter Magyar as Hungary’s future prime minister closely mirrors the one Tusk faced (and still faces): restoring the rule of law in a country that, under nationalist rule, has entered into conflict with EU institutions.

In Magyar’s case, the challenge is arguably even more deman
snippet: The future Hungarian Prime Minister Péter Magyar is in Brussels today for talks with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen on unlocking EU funds. In a comparable situation two years earli
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Why is Gen Z unwilling to fight for their country?
content:
At the ballot box, young Poles from Gen Z demand full sovereignty and increasingly support the radical right. Yet in the face of war, this same generation  —  deeply sceptical of the state  —  firmly rejects the idea of fighting for their homeland. 

As Europe is slowly awakening from its pacifist slumber and governments across the continent are dusting off conscription plans, a growing divide is emerging in Poland. On the one hand, young people — particularly men — are increasingly turning towards the radical right, which promotes slogans of sovereignty and national pride. On the other hand, an overwhelming majority of them openly declare that in the event of war they would pack their bags and leave the country. How can one understand a generation that defends borders on ballot papers but has no intention of defending anything on the front line?

Across the continent, the spectre of war is once again looming. A report by the Research Service of the European Parliament makes it clear that conscription, until recen

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Orban is gone, Radev is here. Has Moscow gained a new friend in the EU?
content:
In April, the elections in Hungary were undoubtedly in the spotlight. Ultimately, they brought an end to the 16-year rule of pro-Kremlin Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and raised hopes for a new beginning in Budapest’s relations with the European Union (EU).

But will this Kremlin ally’s place remain vacant? There are several prominent politicians in Europe whose rhetoric at least partially aligns with Kremlin ideas, and one of them won parliamentary elections in Bulgaria just a week after Victor Orban’s defeat.

Hungary in the spotlight

Hungary’s parliamentary elections attracted enormous media and public attention, as pre-election polls suggested a looming defeat for Orban, who had been in power since 2010 and had become the closest ally of Russian dictator Vladimir Putin within the EU.

In addition to Budapest’s disputes with Brussels over the erosion of democratic norms—leading to the freezing of 18 billion euros in EU subsidies — Orban in recent years distinguished himself by openly accommodating the aggressor state, obstructing sanctions against Moscow and block
snippet: In April, the elections in Hungary were undoubtedly in the spotlight. Ultimately, they brought an end to the 16-year rule of pro-Kremlin Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and raised hopes for a new beginnin
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Can a house and land be taken away in Latvia for not paying taxes?
content:
“You have bought a house or a flat. You have land. But every year you have to pay a “TAX”. If you don’t pay = THEY WILL TAKE IT AWAY. So answer honestly: is it your property, or you just have the right to use it temporarily?” This provocative post was published in April on X by boxer and current Riga City Council member Mairis Briedis.

He was elected from the Stabilitātei! (“For Stability!”) party list, but now sits as an independent councillor.

Mairis Briedis has previously drawn public attention with controversial statements — particularly after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, when he refused to take a clear stance, criticised the media, and later opposed the demolition of the so-called Victory Monument. Meanwhile, the party he once represented is now close to falling apart. In the past, Stabilitātei! had promised voters to abolish real estate tax (RET) for a primary residence.

Briedis told Delfi that by “tax” he meant property tax on land and buildings. If it is not paid for a year or two, the property is “taken away”, he claims. Enforcement proceedings begin after about a yea

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Fact check: was free coffee handed out in Riga during Soviet times?
content:
The social media page “Unusual Riga” has gained considerable popularity, regularly posting various entries that romanticise and beautify the Soviet period. Among them is one publication featuring an AI-generated image in which well-dressed women from Riga, smiling children, and soldiers in Dome Square are enjoying “free coffee” from plastic cups.

The image is fake because real coffee was difficult to obtain during the Soviet times, let alone for free. Meanwhile, the romanticisation of the Soviet era is a tool of propaganda and hybrid warfare, continuing even today to conceal the severe economic, social, and political consequences in the societies of every country occupied by Russia.

The page, under its Russian-language name, can be found both on Facebook and, under its English-language name, on Instagram. On both platforms, various seemingly harmless scenes and drawings from recent history are from time to time mixed with ideologically charged content.

The misleading image of “free coffee” is not the only one that encourages nostalgia for Soviet times. Looking through the “Unusual Riga” accounts on Instagram and Facebook,
snippet: The social media page “Unusual Riga” has gained considerable popularity, regularly posting various entries that romanticise and beautify the Soviet period. Among them is one publication featuring an A
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Can Péter Magyar deliver? Hungary’s EU funding battle reaches crunch point in Brussels
content:
Hungary’s incoming government faces an urgent and complex challenge: securing billions in frozen EU funds before critical deadlines expire. Success would boost both the economy and Péter Magyar’s political standing – failure could prove costly on both fronts.

Even if only by a narrow margin, Péter Magyar and his team may still be able to meet the deadline – and they have little choice if they do not want to forfeit vast amounts of EU funding.

One of Péter Magyar’s key campaign promises was to recover EU funds due to Hungary that have been frozen in several stages since 2020. The absence of these resources has contributed to the country’s economic difficulties, and their recovery could help restart growth and stabilise the budget.

Not least, it would also represent a major political success for Magyar, strengthening his legitimacy and delivering tangible results to voters quickly.

On Wednesday, Magyar will meet Ursula von der Leyen in Brussels to discuss unlocking the funds. This will be the first high-level in-person meeting between the two sides since the 12 Ap
snippet: Hungary’s incoming government faces an urgent and complex challenge: securing billions in frozen EU funds before critical deadlines expire. Success would boost both the economy and Péter Magyar’s poli
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How ”safe” is the Romanian SAFE?
content:
Romania has a minority government without parliamentary backing and it could fall next week, when a vote on a motion of no confidence submitted by the Social Democrats and the far-right is scheduled.

In this complicated context, dark clouds have gathered over the SAFE programme, even though some projects were approved on Tuesday, April 28, in the defence committees of the Chamber of Deputies and the Senate. 

The leader of the far-right AUR party, George Simion, has asked Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan not to sign any major contracts during this period, including in the defence sector, and has warned public officials that they will be held accountable if they sign such contracts.

The motion of no confidence has been signed by 253 MPs, and if all of them vote for it, the government will be dismissed. Although the AUR leader wants to come to power, President Nicușor Dan said on Tuesday, April 28, that none of the pro-European parties wants to form a government with anti-Western forces.

He welcomed the pro-European direction and the responsibility of those who appr
snippet: Romania has a minority government without parliamentary backing and it could fall next week, when a vote on a motion of no confidence submitted by the Social Democrats and the far-right is scheduled.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: The price of war – how the conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East are driving up the costs
content:
The global and European economies have entered a state of permanent crisis, in which geopolitical turmoil has marked the end of the era of cheap energy and secure supply chains.

At the center of these events are the countries of Eastern Europe. The countries in this region, including Bulgaria, are feeling the direct weight of two overlapping global conflicts – the ongoing war in Ukraine and the conflict in the Middle East that erupted in early 2026 between the U.S. and Israel against Iran. The result for the end consumer is clear: a surge in prices for a wide range of goods – from the gas pump to the bread on the table.

The spiralling inflation in the EU began as early as February 2022 with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, which forced Europe to abruptly stop purchasing Russian energy. For Bulgaria, which before the war imported over 90% of its natural gas and about 60% of its oil from Russia, this transition was difficult, but Russia’s share of pipeline gas supplies to the EU has already fallen sharply from about 40% in 2021 to approximately 6% by 2025.

“Proximity cost”

Although markets

title: Who’s paying up? Defence spending on the Eastern Flank [PODCAST]
content:
Tune in to the new episode of the EastFlank Podcast. This time, journalists from newsrooms across countries of NATO’s and the EU’s eastern flank discuss defence spending.


Donald Trump once again puts the issue of defence financing on a knife’s edge, while the European Union steps up its efforts to increase strategic autonomy in this area. We look at how countries on the eastern flank are navigating this complex and challenging reality.

Find out which countries spend the most on defence — and which approach these expenditures with greater scepticism. As always, the discussion is based on the latest EastFlank Report.

The post Who’s paying up? Defence spending on the Eastern Flank [PODCAST] appeared first on Eastflank.
